tags.qa_notes: Re-audited 2026-06-04. HALLUCINATION REMOVED: 'Permanent moorings are attached to the wreck' - FALSE. Government of Grenada specifically requested no permanent moorings; divers do a free negative descent. Deaths confirmed 3 total (1 immediately in explosion + 2 later from burns) per Wikipedia and multiple search sources. Length 180m confirmed. Depth 50m for wreck bottom confirmed; shallowest accessible hull ~28-30m confirmed. Cause: engine room explosion confirmed. Stern lost late 1980s/early 1992 confirmed. All other facts validated.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. CORRECTION: Previous description said '65+ sculptures' - official source (underwatersculpture.com) confirms 75 original works covering 800 sqm, expanded in 2023 with 27 new works ('The Coral Carnival') for 102+ total. Vicissitudes (26 children in a ring, installed 2007) confirmed. Park opened May 2006. Hurricane Ivan 2004 damage context confirmed. Listed as National Geographic's 25 Wonders of the World confirmed. Marine life: no site-specific species confirmed beyond general reef colonisation.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. MARINE LIFE CORRECTED: 'Nurse sharks occasionally found resting under overhangs' removed - not confirmed by primary sources. Confirmed species: chromis, squirrelfish, jackknife fish, Creole wrasse, parrotfish, butterflyfish, grouper, jacks, rays, sea turtles (ECO Dive, PADI). Sandals source replaced with PADI which has site-specific data.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. HALLUCINATION REMOVED: 'Caribbean reef sharks seen regularly at Boss Reef' - primary sources confirm only nurse sharks (occasional). 'Eagle rays cruise the sandy transitions' also removed - no confirmation. Confirmed species: Caribbean lobsters, spotted moray eels, trunkfish, banded coral shrimp, Creole wrasse, turtles, nurse sharks (occasional), stingrays, barracuda, giant green moray. Named sections: Japanese Gardens, Lower Boss, Middle Boss, Northern Exposure, Valleys. Deep hole at 14-18m confirmed as barracuda hotspot. Reef extends ~5 miles from St George's to Point Salines.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. ADDITION: Tug boat was specifically sunk in September 2007 (per PADI source) - not just 'in the shallows.' Confirmed marine life: grouper, French angelfish, chromis, spotted moray eels, snappers on tug wreck (ECO Dive/PADI). Sandy area confirmed as habitat for octopus and rays. Black coral on wall confirmed. Previous nurse shark and octopus claims in tips retained as plausible but noted as unconfirmed.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. Confirmed species from ECO Dive: angelfish, queen triggerfish, grunts, trunkfish, filefish, rays, turtles, barracuda. Steady 2-knot current confirmed. Purple Creole wrasse naming confirmed. Lobsters, moray eels, seahorses in whip corals confirmed (ECO Dive). Marine life status unchanged from prior QA. Eagle rays and Caribbean reef sharks still not confirmed at this specific site.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. Length conflict unresolved: ECO Dive says 120ft cargo barge, DreamWrecks says 130ft, x-ray mag says 40m (~131ft), one-million-places says 40m. Current description uses 130ft from DreamWrecks; x-ray mag (40m=131ft) is consistent with that. Depth: maximum 12m per x-ray mag vs 15m in JSON - sources slightly conflict; keeping 15m per ECO Dive/one-million-places. Marine life: creole wrasse, frogfish, seahorses, French angelfish, damselfish confirmed (dreamwrecks). Black-and-white moray eels also confirmed (one-million-places). Sinking history: 1993, St George's harbour, refloated and moved to Boss Reef confirmed across all sources.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. Length: 180ft confirmed (DreamWrecks, Freedom Tour Travel). Sinking: May 2001, cement cargo shifted in storm, blocked pumps, confirmed across all sources. Depth: DreamWrecks says 110ft, ECO Dive says 55-100ft (16-30m); JSON depth 32m (~105ft) consistent with these. Cement pillows in holds confirmed. Spiral staircase, captain's quarters, bridge, engine room intact confirmed. Marine life: green moray eels, black gorgonians, white hydroids, rainbow sponges confirmed (ECO Dive, DreamWrecks). No tiger/Nassau groupers or Caribbean reef sharks confirmed at this site.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. CRITICAL CORRECTION: Previous description placed this site on 'Grenada's north coast' requiring 30-60 minute boat ride - WRONG. Multiple sources confirm Northern Exposure is part of the Boss Reef system on the SOUTHWEST coast, accessible from St George's. Coordinates corrected from 12.2,-61.62 (northeast coast) to 12.06,-61.77 (southwest coast near St George's, per Tripomatic 12.059N, 61.770W). MARINE LIFE CORRECTED: 'Caribbean reef sharks more common here than south coast sites' and 'occasional hammerhead sharks' removed - not confirmed. Confirmed species: Creole wrasse, porcupine fish, Spanish hogfish, yellowtail snapper, schoolmasters, parrotfish, lobsters, stingrays, conch, octopus, barracuda, Atlantic spadefish, turtles, azure vase sponges. Eel garden at 6m confirmed. Max depth 23m confirmed.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. 'Spice Island Reef' does not appear in any primary source by this exact name. x-ray mag mentions a dive called 'Spice Island Reef' operated by Aquanauts Grenada - a sandy area bordered by reef of finger and brain corals with bommies, juvenile fish, yellowtail snapper, blue chromis, sea plumes. This is consistent with a southwest Grenada shallow reef but the name is not widely used by other operators. Cannot validate with 3+ independent sources - remaining unvalidated. Markdown updated to reflect x-ray mag description. Not enough sources to validate.

Re-audited 2026-06-04. ADDITION: Vehicle graveyard (cars and buses) at 90ft/28m confirmed by PADI and Sportif Dive - adds to description. MARINE LIFE CORRECTED: Previous description had generic marine life; confirmed species added: spotted drums, yellowtail snapper, bara jacks, banded jawfish, lobsters, spotted morays, nurse sharks (sleeping), long-snout seahorses, pipefish. Naming origin confirmed: single whale + calf sighting at 18m. Admiralty anchor at mid-depth confirmed. Drift toward Dragon Bay on tidal current confirmed. Photography section corrected: eagle ray pass references removed (not confirmed). Turq.com source notes this as section of Boss Reef system, calling it 'Valleys of the Whale.'

Re-audited 2026-06-04. 'Happy Hill' does not appear by this name in any primary dive site source checked (ECO Dive, PADI, Pure Grenada, Sportif Dive, DiveIn, ScubaDiving.place, ScubaBoard). Multiple sites reference 'Happy Valley' (= Valley of Whales) as a wall dive, but no separate 'Happy Hill' reef site is found. This may be an invented/composite name. Remaining unvalidated - cannot find 3 independent sources. Markdown description is plausible for a southwest Grenada shallow reef but cannot be attributed to any real named site.
